strava.name: Strava
strava.domain: Sports activity tracking and social fitness
strava.modality: Mobile-first GPS, maps, social feed, route and segment interaction
strava.positioning: Mobile social activity network and multi-sport tracking.
strava.summary: Strava emphasizes broad sports tracking, social competition, route and segment culture, activity sharing, and privacy settings.

nike_run_club.name: Nike Run Club
nike_run_club.modality: Mobile-first run tracking, audio-guided coaching, plans, challenges, achievements, live location sharing, optional Apple Watch support
nike_run_club.positioning: Mobile running coach and guided run experience, with optional Apple Watch support.
nike_run_club.summary: Nike Run Club emphasizes running-specific coaching, audio guidance, beginner support, training plans, achievements, challenges, and safety sharing.

Strava: Campus runner; three 5 km runs per week
Track pace and join monthly distance challenges
Forgets privacy settings when routes start near home
Outdoor campus loop, bright sun, phone in armband, one-handed checks
Compare segment performance and plan familiar routes
Leaderboards can feel punitive when rankings are far away
Road ride planning at home, then mid-ride glances on phone mount
Save walks privately and see progress without public pressure
Discard/save wording feels risky when tired
Evening walk, low light, sweaty hands, intermittent attention
Nike Run Club: Follow guided runs without planning too much
Gets confused when there are many run types and plan options
Campus road, evening run, phone in hand or armband, earphones, outdoor noise, intermittent attention
Follow structured plan, track pace, compare progress, keep streaks
Audio cues and achievement prompts may interrupt focus
Morning urban run, phone locked, earphones, bright sunlight, moving fast, safety concern at crossings
Returns to running after long break
Run safely, avoid overtraining, share live location with family
Unsure whether live sharing, plan difficulty, and achievements match his fitness level
Night neighborhood run, low light, sweaty hands, phone in belt pouch, family wants safety updates

Strava: Open Record, wait for location confidence, start, pause/finish, review, set privacy, save.
Campus loop; evening; standing then moving; phone in armband; mobile data; traffic noise; large touch controls; evidence [2][3].
Open Maps, Create Route, choose sport, tap route points, review distance/elevation, save.
Home planning; seated; Wi-Fi; map touch; route mental model; evidence [4].
Open activity, inspect segment match, compare rank, filter context, decide next training goal.
Post-ride review; indoor light; cognitive comparison; evidence [5].
Browse challenge, inspect goal terms, join, track progress, receive completion feedback.
Review activity, choose visibility, confirm audience, save, verify detail page.
Home-route privacy concern; low light; error prevention; evidence [2][3].
Nike Run Club: Start and record a free run

Trigger: wants a quick run. Preconditions: signed in, location permission enabled. Flow: Open Nike Run Club, Run tab, select basic run or target, press start, watch pace/distance/duration, pause/finish, review result, save. Alternate: set distance/time target first. Error path: location permission missing; app prompts permission and runner restarts. Feedback: countdown, active metrics, pause state, saved run confirmation.
Campus road; evening; standing then jogging; phone in hand or armband; cellular may vary; street noise; thumb touch; postcondition saved free run; evidence [9][11][14].

Trigger: wants coaching. Preconditions: headphones connected and audio available. Flow: choose Guided Run, review coach/run length, connect headphones, start run, receive coach voice and pace cues, glance at core metrics, finish, review achievements, save/share. Alternate: continue without headphones using speaker. Error path: cue missed in traffic; runner needs replay or text summary. Feedback: coach voice, elapsed time, pace cues, completion message.
Campus route; outdoor noise; fatigue; intermittent attention; evidence [9][10][11][14][15].
Choose and follow a training plan

Trigger: race preparation. Preconditions: goal distance and schedule known. Flow: open plans, choose goal, inspect weekly schedule, start plan, complete scheduled run, view progress. Alternate: edit schedule. Error path: beginner picks plan too hard and abandons; needs clearer load preview. Feedback: weekly progress, next run, completion status.
Morning urban training; phone locked during run; calendar mental model; bright sunlight; evidence [10][12][13][15].

Trigger: wants motivation with friends. Preconditions: account and friends/community available. Flow: browse challenge, inspect goal terms and time window, join or invite friends, record runs, view progress screen, receive achievement. Alternate: private challenge. Error path: falling behind creates pressure; needs casual labels. Feedback: mileage progress, rank/status, badges.
Monthly mileage challenge; social motivation and pressure; evidence [9][10][11].
Share live run location and finish safely

Trigger: night run safety. Preconditions: location permission and chosen contacts. Flow: choose live sharing, confirm recipients and duration, start run, family receives location, finish, end sharing, confirm stopped status. Alternate: extend duration. Error path: runner is unsure who can see location; needs receipt and expiry. Feedback: sharing active indicator, stop button, finish confirmation.
Night neighborhood run; low light; sweaty hands; phone in belt pouch; family safety mental model; evidence [10][15].

Strava: Large primary action supports one-handed run start
Motor efficiency; recognition over recall; feedback
Maya can start recording while standing at a crossing without navigating menus.
Destructive discard sits near completion decisions
Lina may discard a walk after fatigue because the end state mixes save and discard choices.
Per-activity visibility can be changed before or after upload
Maya can reduce exposure before saving a home-start route.
Multiple privacy levels require users to understand social reach
Lina may not remember whether followers include local acquaintances.
Mobile route creation uses map interaction and sport type
Dara can sketch a road ride by manipulating the map instead of typing turn-by-turn notes.
Route previews can overload casual planners with metrics
Maya only needs a safe 5 km loop but faces distance, map, and route tradeoffs together.
Leaderboards show comparative performance for matched GPS efforts
Dara sees whether a climb effort improved relative to local riders.
Large leaderboards can demotivate non-elite users
Maya sees a low rank after a good personal run and feels the app ignored her context.
Challenge progress provides visible goal feedback
Maya can see distance remaining and maintain a weekly habit.
Dense post-activity detail supports review but raises attention cost
Lina gets evidence of progress, but small labels and social controls require careful reading.
Nike Run Club: A clear primary start action supports quick free-run recording
Motor efficiency; recognition over recall; feedback
Anh can begin a basic run outside without planning a workout first.
Pace, distance, duration, heart rate or splits where supported
Dense running metrics support progress checks but can compete with road attention
Sofia glances for pace at speed, but reading too many values near a crossing can distract her.
Audio-guided runs reduce visual demand and provide situated coaching
Auditory feedback; reduced visual demand; situated coaching
Anh keeps eyes on the road while hearing when to relax, speed up, or finish.
Cues can be missed when fatigue, music, or traffic masks speech
Hearing limitation; split attention; interruption cost
Sofia misses a pace cue beside traffic and has no low-effort way to recover it.
Plans give structure but can overload beginners choosing distance, schedule, and intensity
Mental model; progressive disclosure; choice overload
Anh wants one first-5K path but must interpret plan options and weekly commitment.
Community challenges motivate mileage but can create comparison pressure
Social motivation; comparison pressure; motivation design
Sofia joins friends, then feels pressure after missing runs during recovery.
Achievements reinforce progress but can overvalue extrinsic reward
Minh likes a first-week badge, but a broken streak can discourage needed rest.
Real-time sharing supports safety and trust during outdoor runs
Visibility of system status; safety; trust
Minh can let family track his route while he runs in low light.
Sharing setup can create privacy uncertainty if audience and expiry are not salient
Privacy mental model; error prevention; system status
Minh is unsure who sees location and whether sharing stops when the run ends.
Optional Apple Watch support improves glanceability but needs consistent mode visibility across devices
Sofia can start or monitor a run with less phone handling, then review details later on the phone.

Strava: Large record/start path reduces setup time.
Goal feedback makes effort visible over time.
Nike Run Club: A simple start flow supports first-run confidence.
Voice guidance lowers visual demand during motion.
Plans convert race goals into weekly action.
Live location sharing increases trust for night runs.

Strava: End-of-activity decisions can cause accidental data loss.
Users may not understand who can see a sensitive route.
Map, sport, distance, and elevation choices can overload casual users.
Broad rankings can demotivate personal-progress users.
Stats, map, comments, and sharing controls compete for attention.
Nike Run Club: Audio-guided run cues may be missed in noisy outdoor contexts.
Traffic noise, earphone issues, music playback, and fatigue can mask instructions for Anh, Sofia, and Minh.
Training plan selection may overload beginners.
First 5K, return-from-break, and low-confidence contexts can make plan choice feel risky for Anh and Minh.
Challenge and achievement mechanics may pressure casual users.
Falling behind a challenge, streak loss, and rest-day anxiety can reduce motivation for Anh and Minh.
Live location sharing may create privacy uncertainty.
Night-run safety sharing can create uncertainty about duration and recipients for Sofia and Minh.
During-run metric density may distract runners in motion.
Bright sunlight, fast pace, road crossings, and sweaty hands can make metric inspection unsafe for Sofia and Anh.

solutions: After discard, move activity to a 7-day trash with Restore and Delete forever.
Expected gain: fewer lost recordings. Tradeoff: storage and extra state. Priority High. Effort Medium.
Make Save primary, move Discard behind a confirm sheet with activity summary.
Expected gain: fewer accidental destructive actions. Tradeoff: one extra tap for true discard. Priority High. Effort Low.
Show audience, map visibility, and home-area warning before save.
Expected gain: higher confidence. Tradeoff: more review text. Priority High. Effort Low.
Detect route near saved home area and suggest Only You or hidden map zone.
Expected gain: lower accidental exposure. Tradeoff: false positives. Priority High. Effort Medium.
Ask distance, sport, and loop/out-back, then recommend one editable route.
Expected gain: lower setup effort. Tradeoff: less initial control. Priority Medium. Effort Medium.
Label routes Easy, Hilly, Unfamiliar, or Busy based on visible criteria.
Expected gain: faster decision. Tradeoff: requires clear criteria. Priority Medium. Effort Medium.
Default casual users to similar-level comparison bands before global ranking.
Expected gain: less demotivation. Tradeoff: competitive users may prefer global view. Priority Medium. Effort High.
Show PR delta and trend before rank for casual profiles.
Expected gain: healthier motivation. Tradeoff: rank is one tap deeper. Priority Medium. Effort Low.
Show map, distance, time, privacy first; hide comments/segments behind tabs.
Expected gain: less visual overload. Tradeoff: advanced metrics are less immediate. Priority Medium. Effort Medium.
Offer high-contrast, large-type post-run summary for tired or low-light contexts.
Expected gain: better readability. Tradeoff: more display modes. Priority Low. Effort Medium.
Small accessible control on run screen and headphone gesture shortcut; shows last cue text summary for 5 seconds.
Recovery; auditory limitation support; reduced memory load
Expected gain: fewer missed instructions. Tradeoff: adds control to run screen. Priority High. Effort Low.
User selects Quiet route, Normal, or Noisy street; app increases cue clarity, haptic emphasis, and text summary by mode.
Expected gain: better guidance in traffic. Tradeoff: mode setup can add complexity. Priority Medium. Effort High.
Ask current ability, target distance, available days, injury concern; recommend one plan with a clear reason.
Expected gain: lower setup effort. Tradeoff: users may want full list immediately. Priority High. Effort Medium.
Before starting a plan, show weekly load, rest days, expected longest run, and Edit Difficulty.
Expected gain: lower overcommitment. Tradeoff: more pre-plan reading. Priority Medium. Effort Medium.
Rest day counts as protected training when user selects recovery.
Expected gain: less pressure to run every day. Tradeoff: streak semantics change. Priority Medium. Effort Medium.
Label challenges Casual, Consistent, Competitive; default new users to Casual.
Expected gain: better fit by user type. Tradeoff: challenge taxonomy maintenance. Priority Medium. Effort Low.
After starting sharing, show recipients, duration, stop button, and finish notification status.
Visibility of system status; privacy mental model
Expected gain: higher trust. Tradeoff: extra status surface. Priority High. Effort Low.
Default live sharing expires at run end or chosen duration; end screen confirms sharing stopped.
Expected gain: lower privacy risk. Tradeoff: edge cases for extended runs. Priority Medium. Effort Medium.
During movement, show only distance, pace, and elapsed time with large type; move other metrics behind swipe or post-run detail.
Cognitive load reduction; human vision; motor efficiency
Expected gain: safer glance behavior. Tradeoff: fewer metrics visible by default. Priority High. Effort Medium.
If phone detects fast movement and screen wake, delay nonessential overlays and use voice or haptic cues.
Expected gain: fewer distraction moments. Tradeoff: may suppress desired messages. Priority Medium. Effort High.

sources.title: Strava | Running, Cycling & Hiking App
claims_supported: Strava positions itself around activity tracking, maps, performance data, and community sharing.
sources.notes: Official source; accessed for PA1 evidence refresh.

sources.title: Recording an Activity - Strava Support
claims_supported: Mobile recording includes activity capture, save/discard choices, activity details, privacy controls, and Beacon entry points.
sources.notes: Official source; accessed for PA1 evidence refresh.

sources.title: Activity Privacy Controls - Strava Support
claims_supported: Activities can use Everyone, Followers, or Only You visibility, with default and per-activity changes.
sources.notes: Official source; accessed for PA1 evidence refresh.

sources.title: Creating Routes on Mobile - Strava Support
claims_supported: The mobile Maps tab and Create Route flow support route planning by sport type and map interaction.
sources.notes: Official source; accessed for PA1 evidence refresh.

sources.title: Segment Leaderboard Guidelines - Strava Support
claims_supported: Segments use matched GPS efforts and leaderboards separated by activity type.
sources.notes: Official source; accessed for PA1 evidence refresh.

sources.title: Strava Challenges - Strava Support
claims_supported: Challenges motivate activity through distance, elevation, time, segment, and frequency goals.
sources.notes: Official source; accessed for PA1 evidence refresh.

claims_supported: Nike describes pace, location, distance, elevation, heart rate, mile splits, progress history, and wearable pairing.

assumptions: GROUP_ID remains the default placeholder because no real group identifier was provided.
Team roster uses placeholder names because no real member names were provided.
The weekly-report template PDF was not present in this repository or /mnt/data during repair, so the fallback RUP + Scrum structure is used.
Access date is recorded as 2026-06-10 using the workspace date.
Interface observations are source-grounded from official product/help/store/newsroom pages, not from unsupported screenshots.
Mermaid source is preserved and readable text fallback diagrams are included in PDFs.
